The rationale for sharing business logic (domain + data layers) while keeping UI platform-native is that users expect platform conventions—iOS users expect swipe-to-dismiss on NavigationView stacks, Android users expect Material 3 design language. The pure KMM approach (shared logic, native UI) provides higher quality UX than the Compose Multiplatform approach (shared UI) at the cost of implementing features twice, which is acceptable for apps where UX quality is a key differentiator.

Implementing content providers for sharing data between Android apps uses a custom ContentProvider backed by Room, with proper URI permissions and read/write permission declarations in the manifest. The assembly for a files content provider is: declare provider with android:authorities='com.example.app.provider', android:exported='true', android:readPermission='com.example.app.READ', use UriMatcher to route URIs to table/row queries, and grant per-URI permissions dynamically with Context.grantUriPermission() rather than the declaring broad permissions. The critical security consideration is that ContentProviders are accessible to all apps on the device when exported=true without proper permission declarations—a misconfigured ContentProvider is a classic data exfiltration vulnerability.

Android's foreground service requires calling startForeground() within 5 seconds of the service being started, or the system throws a ForegroundServiceDidNotStartInTimeException (Android 12+) or ANR (earlier versions). The hidden failure pattern is starting the foreground service from an Activity, performing some async initialization (loading from DataStore, network health check) before calling startForeground(), and occasionally taking more than 5 seconds on slow devices. The correct pattern is calling startForeground() immediately in Service.onCreate() with a minimal notification, then updating the notification content after async initialization completes.

Implementing a health-grade step counter in Android uses the ACTIVITY_RECOGNITION permission with SensorManager for real-time step data or the Health Connect API (Jetpack Health Connect) for historical fitness data that aggregates across fitness apps. The assembly for Health Connect is: request health connect permissions via ActivityResultContract, use HealthConnectClient.readRecords(ReadRecordsRequest(StepsRecord::class, TimeRangeFilter.between(start, end))) to query step data, and request data through the Health Connect app's unified permission model. Direct SensorManager use with TYPE_STEP_COUNTER provides cumulative steps since reboot and requires ACTIVITY_RECOGNITION permission on API 29+.

The Android Gradle Plugin 8.0+ requires all dependencies to have explicit namespace declarations and deprecated the use of android.applicationId in non-app modules. The rationale for the namespace requirement is that namespace determines the R class package name, and relying on applicationId created ambiguity when app variants had different applicationId values. The practical impact is that every library and feature module in a multi-module project must have an android.namespace set in its build.gradle.kts—migrating large existing projects requires adding namespace to every module's build file, and the old manifest package attribute is no longer the source of truth for R class generation.

Jetpack Compose's ModalBottomSheet from Material3 does not persist state across back stack navigation by default—the sheet's content is recreated every time it is shown, losing any scroll position or form input. The failure is implementing a multi-page form inside a ModalBottomSheet where users can swipe down to dismiss and reopen—all form input is lost on dismissal. The correct approach is either hosting the ModalBottomSheet's state in the parent ViewModel (so it survives sheet dismissal), or using a Navigation Compose dialog destination with a custom sliding animation to achieve the same UX while participating in the navigation back stack.

Implementing proper crash symbolication for Android requires uploading the mapping.txt file (R8 obfuscation map) to Firebase Crashlytics or the crash reporting service for every release build. The CI pipeline must archive mapping.txt as a build artifact and upload it as part of the release workflow. Without the mapping file, Crashlytics shows obfuscated stack traces with renamed class and method names that are unreadable. The AGP Gradle plugin can be configured to automatically upload mapping files with crashlyticsUploadMappingFileRelease task, but this requires the Crashlytics Gradle plugin applied alongside the Firebase BoM dependency.

Android's process death and state restoration require every screen's UI state to be restorable from a Bundle or saved state. The constraint is that the Android OS can kill the app process when it is in the background to reclaim memory, and when the user returns to the app via the task switcher, the system restores the Activity stack but not the in-memory ViewModel state. ViewModels are not recreated from process death—only SavedStateHandle data survives process death via the saved state mechanism. This means any UI state that must survive process death must be stored in SavedStateHandle, which only supports Bundle-compatible types.

Kotlin's suspend functions with retrofit2-kotlin-coroutines-adapter or Retrofit 2.6+ native suspend support enables writing network calls as straightforward suspend fun getUser(id: String): User without callback or RxJava wrapper overhead. The rationale for suspend-based Retrofit over RxJava in new Kotlin projects is that suspend functions integrate naturally with structured concurrency—canceling the calling coroutine automatically cancels the in-flight Retrofit request through OkHttp's Call.cancel(). RxJava's Observable chains require explicit Disposable management and CompositeDisposable cleanup, which is error-prone and conceptually different from the coroutines mental model.

Implementing secure inter-process communication in Android beyond ContentProvider uses AIDL (Android Interface Definition Language) for bidirectional communication with custom Parcelable objects, or Binder directly for simpler use cases. The assembly for a bound service with AIDL is: define the .aidl interface file, implement the Stub in the Service, bind from the client with ServiceConnection, cast the IBinder with IMyService.Stub.asInterface(binder). The security discipline is declaring proper android:permission on the <service> declaration so only authorized clients can bind, and validating caller UID with Binder.getCallingUid() for sensitive operations that should be further restricted within the permission-checked IPC boundary.

Android's NetworkOnMainThreadException is thrown when calling network operations synchronously on the main thread, but okhttp3 Call.execute() called from a coroutine on Dispatchers.IO does NOT throw NetworkOnMainThreadException—it is only thrown when called from the main thread's looper. The subtle failure is developers who see coroutines in their code and assume all network calls are safe from main thread violations, then call execute() synchronously inside a synchronized block on the main thread for a quick check, triggering the exception in production. StrictMode with detectNetwork() in debug builds catches these violations before they reach production.

Implementing a feature flag system in Android for A/B testing and gradual rollouts uses Firebase Remote Config as the backend. The assembly is: define default values in remote_config_defaults.xml, fetch with remoteConfig.fetchAndActivate() coroutine on app startup with minimum fetch interval (default 12h in production, 0 for debug), read flags with remoteConfig.getBoolean('feature_flag_name'). The production discipline is always providing in-app default values that represent the safe (off) state, so network failures during remote config fetch do not silently enable features for users where the default was misconfigured as the on state.

Android's AppCompat library (androidx.appcompat) backports Material Design components and modern API features to older Android versions, and AppCompatActivity is the base class that enables this compatibility layer. The rationale for always subclassing AppCompatActivity rather than Activity is that AppCompat provides ActionBar/Toolbar support, vector drawable backport, night mode (DayNight themes), and the modern fragment transaction APIs on all supported API levels. Without AppCompat, apps using vector drawables crash below API 21, and Material Design components like MaterialButton and TextInputLayout only work inside AppCompatActivity-derived contexts.

Android's View.post() and View.postDelayed() are not lifecycle-aware—callbacks posted to the main queue execute even after the Activity or Fragment is destroyed, causing IllegalStateException or NPE when the callback references destroyed views. The failure is using view.postDelayed({ updateUI() }, 500) for a brief animation delay, then having the user navigate away before the delay completes—the callback fires 500ms later on the destroyed Fragment. The correct replacement is a coroutine launched in viewLifecycleOwner.lifecycleScope with delay(500) which is automatically cancelled when the lifecycle drops below STARTED.

Implementing widget support for Android 12+ home screen widgets uses Glance (Jetpack Glance) which brings Compose-like API to RemoteViews-based widgets. The assembly is: extend GlanceAppWidget, override provideGlance() with setContent { GlanceTheme { MyWidgetContent() } }, register GlanceAppWidgetReceiver in manifest. Glance composables are limited—only the Glance-specific versions of Row, Column, Text, Image, and Button work (standard Compose components are not supported). Widget updates are triggered by calling MyWidget().update(context, glanceId) where glanceId is obtained from GlanceAppWidgetManager, typically from WorkManager periodic work or FCM push.

Kotlin's coroutine supervisorScope is necessary when multiple parallel operations should proceed independently despite individual failures, but using supervisorScope incorrectly by nesting it inside a regular coroutineScope causes the supervisor semantics to be overridden by the parent scope. The failure is launching N parallel network requests with supervisorScope and expecting that if one fails, the others complete—but if the supervisorScope itself is wrapped in a regular scope from ViewModel, an exception in one request cancels the supervisorScope and propagates to cancel all sibling requests. SupervisorJob in the CoroutineScope constructor is the correct place to establish supervisor semantics at the scope level.

Implementing runtime permissions with a rationale dialog in Jetpack Compose follows a three-state flow: request permission, check if rationale should be shown (shouldShowRequestPermissionRationale()), and handle permanent denial. The assembly using Accompanist Permissions (now replaced by the permissions API in lifecycle-compose) is: val permissionState = rememberPermissionState(CAMERA); when permissionState.status is Denied and shouldShowRationale=true show a rationale dialog before re-requesting; when shouldShowRationale=false the user permanently denied, show settings deep-link dialog with Intent(Settings.ACTION_APPLICATION_DETAILS_SETTINGS). A systematic permission request flow prevents the unrecoverable state where users permanently block permissions without understanding why they are needed.

Android's Paging 3 library's PagingSource uses an append/prepend/refresh load strategy rather than simple page offset arithmetic because this model handles real-world feed behaviors: items deleted from the beginning shift all page offsets, but append/prepend keys based on item IDs or cursors remain stable. The rationale for key-based pagination (nextKey = lastItemId) over offset-based pagination (nextKey = page + 1) is that offset-based pagination silently shows duplicate or missing items when the backend list changes between page requests. Cursor-based keyset pagination is the correct backend API design for any list that changes frequently, and Paging 3 PagingSource models this directly.

Android's Keystore does not support key export—once a key is generated in the Keystore, it cannot be extracted in any form, including backup. The constraint means that data encrypted with a Keystore key is permanently lost if the device is factory reset, the key is deleted, or the key becomes invalid (user fingerprint removal invalidates biometric-required keys). Applications that encrypt user data with Keystore keys must implement a separate backup encryption mechanism (user-provided passphrase encrypting a symmetric key that is stored in cloud backup) to avoid data loss on device replacement. This architectural decision must be made before first release—retrofitting backup support after users have stored data is a migration challenge.

Implementing a search suggestion feature in Android with SearchView uses SearchRecentSuggestionsProvider backed by SearchRecentSuggestions for history-based suggestions, or a custom SuggestionsAdapter with a FilterQueryProvider for real-time suggestions. For Compose, the SearchBar and SearchView Material3 components integrate with a ViewModel that debounces the query input (debounce(300ms) on the query Flow before triggering search) to avoid excessive network calls on each keystroke. The debounce operator with distinctUntilChanged() before the flatMapLatest network call ensures that only the final stable query string after 300ms of no input triggers a network request, canceling any in-flight requests from intermediate keystrokes.

Android's Drawable resource loading with ResourcesCompat.getDrawable() is not thread-safe before API 21 and produces occasional crashes on older devices when called from background threads. On API 21+ the Resources class is thread-safe, but Vector Drawables loaded via AppCompatResources.getDrawable() still require the main thread for the initial inflation due to VectorDrawableCompat not being thread-safe. The failure is loading drawables in a background coroutine as part of a data processing pipeline and using them to annotate Spans on a SpannableString, then crashing only on specific older devices. All drawable loading must be performed on the main thread or cached as bitmaps that can be used cross-thread.

Kotlin's Flow.shareIn() converts a cold flow to a hot SharedFlow, enabling multiple collectors to receive the same emissions without each triggering a new upstream collection. The rationale for using shareIn in the repository layer for expensive cold flows (database queries, network polling) is that multiple ViewModels or UI components collecting the same flow independently would each trigger their own database subscription or network connection. shareIn with SharingStarted.WhileSubscribed(5000) keeps the upstream active as long as at least one downstream collector exists, with a 5-second grace period to survive brief lifecycle pauses. This eliminates redundant upstream work while maintaining efficient resource usage.

Implementing app-level error handling in Android uses a sealed ErrorType hierarchy that maps infrastructure errors to user-facing messages through a resource-based mapping layer. The assembly pattern is: define sealed class AppError with NetworkError(cause: Throwable), AuthError, ServerError(code: Int), UnknownError; in the repository catch Throwable and map to AppError; in ViewModel handle AppError and map to StringResource (R.string.error_network) through an ErrorMapper; in UI display the string. This separation ensures that error message localization is in string resources (not in the domain layer), and error handling is testable without Android dependencies by testing the ErrorMapper class with pure JVM tests.

Google Play's 512MB APK size limit and Play Asset Delivery's 2GB asset pack limit require large-content apps (games, offline-capable apps with large datasets) to use Play Asset Delivery or Expansion Files (.obb). The practical constraint for content apps is that on-demand asset packs downloaded after install are subject to network availability and user storage, requiring graceful degradation when assets are not yet available. FastFollow and OnDemand asset packs require Play Core Asset Delivery API for download management and progress reporting, and are unavailable for sideloaded APKs, meaning the download logic must have a fallback for non-Play distributions.

Android's Text-to-Speech engine initialization is asynchronous—calling tts.speak() before the onInit() callback fires produces a queue of utterances that may not play, or throws an IllegalArgumentException on some devices. The correct pattern is initializing TextToSpeech in a suspended coroutine using suspendCoroutine { continuation -> TextToSpeech(context) { status -> continuation.resume(status) } } and only calling speak() after initialization completes with SUCCESS status. On devices without a TTS engine installed (common on low-end Android Go devices), TextToSpeech returns ERROR status and speak() calls silently fail without any exception.

Implementing a document scanner in Android uses ML Kit's Document Scanner API which provides a built-in scanning UI handling perspective correction, quality detection, and PDF generation. The assembly is: DocumentScannerOptions.Builder with scanning mode (FULL or BASE), page limit, and result format (JPEG, PDF); launch with ScannerClient.getStartScanningIntent() via ActivityResultLauncher; handle result with GmsDocumentScanningResult.fromActivityResultIntent(data). For apps requiring custom scanning UI, CameraX with an ImageAnalysis use case passes frames to ML Kit's document detection model. The built-in scanner UI is preferred for most use cases because it handles camera permission, torch control, and quality guidance automatically.

Kotlin's withTimeout() and withTimeoutOrNull() are the correct mechanisms for setting deadlines on coroutine operations, preferred over manually launched cancellation jobs. The rationale is that withTimeout throws TimeoutCancellationException (a subclass of CancellationException) which is handled by structured concurrency, while a manually managed cancellation job must be explicitly canceled in a finally block. The practical difference is that withTimeout composes naturally with other coroutine operators—it can be nested inside supervisorScope to isolate timeout failures, while manual cancellation requires careful resource cleanup logic. The default OkHttp timeouts are separate from coroutine timeouts; both may be needed for defense in depth.

Implementing accessible custom Compose components requires using Modifier.semantics {} to provide meaningful accessibility information beyond what Compose infers from the visual representation. The assembly for a custom toggle button is: Modifier.semantics { role = Role.Switch; toggleableState = ToggleableState(isChecked); contentDescription = if (isChecked) 'Enabled' else 'Disabled' }.clickable { onToggle() }. Without explicit semantics, TalkBack reads custom components as 'unlabeled button' which is unintelligible. Testing accessibility semantics in Compose uses composeTestRule.onNodeWithContentDescription('Enabled').assertIsDisplayed() to verify semantic labels are present without running on a real device with TalkBack.

Android's Gson TypeAdapter for generic types must use TypeToken to preserve generic type information at runtime because Java type erasure removes generic parameters from Class objects. The failure is writing GsonBuilder().registerTypeAdapter(List::class.java, myAdapter) when myAdapter is designed for List<MyItem>—this registers the adapter for all lists regardless of element type. The correct approach is GsonBuilder().registerTypeAdapter(object : TypeToken<List<MyItem>>(){}.type, myAdapter) which registers the adapter for the specific parameterized type. Without TypeToken, Gson falls back to LinkedTreeMap for unknown object types, producing ClassCastException at the first property access.

Implementing screen recording and screenshot prevention for sensitive app screens uses WindowManager.LayoutParams.FLAG_SECURE applied to the Activity window. The assembly is window.setFlags(FLAG_SECURE, FLAG_SECURE) in onCreate() before setContentView(), or conditionally applying it per-screen by calling window.clearFlags(FLAG_SECURE) on screens that are not sensitive. FLAG_SECURE prevents both the built-in screenshot button from capturing the screen and screen recording apps from capturing the window content. The flag also prevents the system task switcher from showing a screenshot of the app's content in the recent apps list, displaying a blank window thumbnail instead.

The Jetpack Compose slot API pattern (passing @Composable lambdas as parameters) enables flexible, reusable UI components where callers control content at specific 'slots' without the component needing to know about the content's type. The rationale is that Android's XML views achieved this with custom attributes and attribute parsing, which is verbose and type-unsafe. Compose's content: @Composable () -> Unit parameters are type-safe, composition-aware, and avoid the View adapter pattern entirely. The Scaffold composable demonstrates the slot pattern with topBar, bottomBar, floatingActionButton, and content slots, each accepting arbitrary composable content.

Android's Room database accessed from multiple processes (app + a WorkManager process running in :worker process isolation) causes database corruption because SQLite in WAL mode requires all connections to be in the same process for proper transaction coordination. The failure is a production app with WorkManager configured with useProcessIsolation=true that writes to Room in the background process while the UI process also writes, producing corrupted data detectable only through DatabaseCorruptException in crash reports. WorkManager should be configured without process isolation when it shares a Room database with the main process, or a separate database per process should be used.

Implementing contactless NFC reading in Android uses NfcAdapter with foreground dispatch to capture NFC intent while the app is in the foreground, preventing the default tag dispatch system from launching other apps. The assembly is: NfcAdapter.enableForegroundDispatch(activity, pendingIntent, intentFiltersArray, techListsArray) in onResume() and NfcAdapter.disableForegroundDispatch(activity) in onPause(). For reading NDEF-formatted tags, parse the NdefMessage from the intent with NdefMessage(rawMessages[0]).records and decode the payload based on the NDEF type (well-known, MIME, external). NFC availability must be checked with NfcManager.getDefaultAdapter() returning non-null before attempting any NFC operations.

Android Automotive OS has significantly different UI guidelines from standard Android—apps must support 5-meter UX (legible from 5 meters), large touch targets, and reduced interaction complexity for driver safety. The constraint is that Play Store for Android Automotive requires separate app verification for the Automotive category, and apps using standard mobile navigation (Navigation Drawer, complex menus) fail automotive quality review. Car App Library provides the abstractions for compliance—CarAppService, Session, Screen hierarchy with templates—which enforce the safety restrictions programmatically and prevent non-compliant layouts from compiling.

Kotlin's Flow.flatMapLatest() cancels and restarts the inner flow on each new emission, which is correct for search queries but catastrophic for transactional operations like payment processing. The failure is using flatMapLatest for a payment processing flow where rapid user taps trigger multiple payment requests—flatMapLatest cancels the first request and starts a new one, potentially leaving the first transaction in an indeterminate state on the server. For operations where every emission must complete, flatMapConcat (queues sequentially) or flatMapMerge with concurrency=1 (same as flatMapConcat but more explicit) are the correct operators. Payment and state-changing API calls must always use flatMapConcat or explicit deduplication logic.

Implementing a proper audio focus management for media playback uses AudioManager with AudioFocusRequest to handle ducking, pausing on phone calls, and competing with other audio apps. The assembly is: AudioFocusRequest.Builder(AUDIOFOCUS_GAIN).setOnAudioFocusChangeListener { focusChange -> handleFocusChange(focusChange) }.setAudioAttributes(audioAttributes).build(); call AudioManager.requestAudioFocus(focusRequest) and only start playback on AUDIOFOCUS_REQUEST_GRANTED. In the focus change listener, pause on AUDIOFOCUS_LOSS, duck (lower volume) on AUDIOFOCUS_LOSS_TRANSIENT_CAN_DUCK, and resume on AUDIOFOCUS_GAIN. Media apps that ignore audio focus play over phone calls and other media, which is a severe UX and compliance violation for Google Play.

Android's Splash Screen API (API 31+, backported via androidx.core.splashscreen) standardizes the app launch experience by showing the app icon on a colored background during the window drawing warm-up phase, preventing the brief white flash. The rationale for adopting the Splash Screen API over custom splash screen activities is that custom splash activities add an extra Activity to the cold start time rather than replacing the system-provided launch animation. The API's windowSplashScreenAnimatedIcon attribute supports animated AVDs for brands that want animated logos. The critical migration note is removing any existing SplashActivity and setting the splash screen theme on the main Activity instead.

Implementing geofencing in Android uses the Geofencing API with GeofencingClient.addGeofences() to register circular regions with entry/exit/dwell triggers. The assembly is: create Geofence objects with setRequestId, setCircularRegion(lat, lng, radius), setTransitionTypes(GEOFENCE_TRANSITION_ENTER|EXIT), setExpirationDuration; create GeofencingRequest with setInitialTrigger; add with GeofencingClient.addGeofences(request, geofencePendingIntent). The background location permission (ACCESS_BACKGROUND_LOCATION) is required for geofences to trigger when the app is in the background. Geofences are limited to 100 per app and are automatically removed on device reboot, requiring re-registration in a boot completed BroadcastReceiver or WorkManager.

Android's ActivityResultContracts.TakePicture() returns a boolean success flag, not the image URI or Bitmap—the image is written to the URI that was passed as input to the contract. The hidden failure is not creating the file URI before launching the contract, then attempting to read from a null URI when the result arrives. The correct pattern is: create a file URI with FileProvider.getUriForFile() first, store it in the ViewModel's SavedStateHandle (to survive process death during the camera app), pass it to launch(), and read from the same URI when success=true is returned. Storing the URI in a regular variable loses it if the system kills the process while the camera is open.

Implementing Material 3 dynamic color theming (Material You) in Android 12+ uses DynamicColorTheme derived from the device's wallpaper palette through DynamicColors.applyIfAvailable(activity) or dynamicColorScheme(context) in Compose. The Compose assembly is: val colorScheme = if (Build.VERSION.SDK_INT >= 31 && dynamicColorAvailable) dynamicLightColorScheme(context) else lightColorScheme(primary=brandColor). Dynamic color must always have a fallback brand-color scheme for Android versions below 12. The Material 3 color role system (primary, secondary, tertiary, surface, error) with their on-color and container variants provides 26 color roles that must all be populated in the custom scheme for consistent theming across all component states.

Google Play Integrity API (replacement for SafetyNet Attestation) requires a server-side nonce and server-side verdict verification—the integrity token returned to the client must never be trusted by the client itself. The constraint is that integrity verdicts (deviceRecognitionVerdict, appIntegrity, accountDetails) are signed by Google and must be verified against Google's public key on the server, not in the app binary. Apps that perform client-side integrity checks are trivially bypassed by patching the APK. The server must request the verdict within 10 minutes of the nonce being generated, and each nonce can only be used once to prevent replay attacks.

Implementing a robust logging system for production Android apps uses Timber with custom Trees—a DebugTree for logcat output in debug builds and a CrashlyticsTree that sends log messages to Crashlytics as breadcrumbs in release builds. The assembly is: Timber.plant(DebugTree()) in Application.onCreate() only for DEBUG build type, Timber.plant(CrashlyticsTree()) always for crash context. CrashlyticsTree.log() calls Crashlytics.log(priority, tag, message) which records the message as a breadcrumb visible in crash reports. The discipline is never using Log.d/Log.e directly—always Timber—and configuring ProGuard rules to strip Timber.d calls from release builds using -assumenosideeffects.

Android's MediaStore insert for saving photos to the gallery requires passing ContentValues with MediaStore.Images.Media.IS_PENDING=1, writing the file content, then updating IS_PENDING=0 to make the file visible to other apps. Skipping the IS_PENDING protocol causes partially written images to appear in the gallery during write, and another app or the media scanner reading the incomplete file may cache a corrupt version. On API 29+, IS_PENDING is also the mechanism that ensures atomic media insertion—without it, the media scanner may index the file before writing is complete, producing 0-byte or corrupted gallery entries visible to users.

Implementing real-time collaboration features in Android uses Firebase Realtime Database or Firestore with snapshot listeners that emit on every change. The Compose assembly with Firestore is: create a Flow<DocumentSnapshot> from addSnapshotListener using callbackFlow { val listenerRegistration = firestore.document(path).addSnapshotListener { snapshot, error -> if (error != null) close(error) else trySend(snapshot!!) }; awaitClose { listenerRegistration.remove() } }. The awaitClose block is critical—without it, the Firestore listener is never removed when the Flow collection is cancelled, causing a memory leak where Firestore continues pushing updates to a disconnected listener.

Android's Jetpack Macrobenchmark library is the correct tool for measuring startup time, scrolling performance, and UI jank in a production-like configuration on real devices. The rationale for using Macrobenchmark over manual profiling is that it runs the app in a compiled (AOT) state matching production and measures across multiple iterations with statistical analysis, while Android Studio profiler sessions run in a JIT-compiled debug build that is 30-50% slower. Macrobenchmark results in milliseconds can be tracked in CI over time to detect performance regressions before they ship to users. The companion Baseline Profiles generation workflow uses Macrobenchmark to capture the hot paths for AOT compilation.

Android's ExoPlayer / Media3 requires all player operations (prepare, play, pause, seek, release) to happen on the same thread, and the default configuration uses the main thread. The hidden failure is creating an ExoPlayer instance in a background thread's coroutine scope, then calling player.prepare() from a different thread, producing an UnsupportedOperationException. The correct architecture is creating ExoPlayer in a ViewModel using Dispatchers.Main scope and holding it as a ViewModel property that is released in onCleared(). The player release in ViewModel.onCleared() is critical—failing to release ExoPlayer when the ViewModel is cleared causes audio to continue playing after the screen is navigated away from.

Implementing offline-capable data sync with conflict resolution uses a last-write-wins strategy with server timestamps or a vector clock approach for complex multi-user scenarios. The assembly pattern for last-write-wins with Firestore is: every document includes updatedAt server timestamp, Firestore offline persistence caches local writes, conflicts resolved by Firestore's server-side timestamp comparison. For client-side conflict detection, attach an etag/version field to each document and use Firestore transactions that read the current version before writing—if the version changed, reject and re-fetch before retrying. Room is the local cache truth, Firestore is the remote truth, and the sync worker reconciles with a defined conflict resolution policy.

Kotlin's operator overloading for common mathematical operations (plus, minus, times) on domain objects like Money or Vector2D makes domain logic readable as mathematical expressions without losing type safety. The rationale for operator overloading in domain models is that totalPrice = items.sumOf { it.price } reads naturally when price is a Money value class with a sumOf extension—versus totalPrice = Money(items.sumOf { it.price.cents }) which exposes the internal representation. The constraint is that operator overloading should be used only when the semantics are universally understood (addition of money amounts, vector addition) and avoided for non-obvious semantics that require documentation to understand.

Implementing an in-app review prompt using Google Play In-App Review API requires careful timing—showing the prompt too early or too often degrades user experience and the OS throttles the dialog if shown too frequently. The assembly is: val reviewManager = ReviewManagerFactory.create(context); reviewManager.requestReviewFlow().addOnCompleteListener { task -> if (task.isSuccessful) reviewManager.launchReviewFlow(activity, task.result) }. The critical discipline is not triggering the prompt in onboarding—trigger it only after the user has completed a meaningful action demonstrating app value (finished a workout, exported a report, sent their 5th message). The system may not show the dialog even when launchReviewFlow() is called, as Google controls display frequency.

Android's InputMethodManager soft keyboard show/hide is notoriously unreliable across OEM implementations. The correct approach using WindowInsetsControllerCompat.show(WindowInsetsCompat.Type.ime()) / hide() is more reliable than InputMethodManager.showSoftInput() and InputMethodManager.hideSoftInputFromWindow() but still not 100% consistent across all devices. The constraint is that timing of keyboard show/hide relative to layout animations causes layout glitches on some Samsung devices where the keyboard overlaps content. Android 11+ introduced IME inset animations via WindowInsetsAnimationCompat that enable synchronizing keyboard slide animations with the UI layout to eliminate overlap glitches.

Room's @Query with LIKE '%:searchQuery%' does not work as intended because Room does not automatically add the wildcard characters when the query is parameterized—the :searchQuery parameter is substituted literally including any wildcards in the parameter value. The correct approach is either: passing the query string with wildcards included at the call site ('%' + query + '%'), or using Room FTS4/FTS5 MATCH operator for full-text search. The failure is a search feature that returns no results because the SQL becomes LIKE '%user_query%' which is interpreted as LIKE the literal string '%user_query%' rather than a pattern. Room does not generate a compile-time error for this mistake.

Kotlin's coroutine-based testing with runTest uses a TestCoroutineScheduler that controls virtual time, allowing delay() calls to complete instantly without real wall clock time. The rationale for using runTest over runBlocking in unit tests is that runTest integrates with Kotlin's coroutine testing infrastructure—it catches uncaught exceptions from launched coroutines, advances virtual time for delay() calls, and ensures all pending coroutines complete before the test assertion runs. Using runBlocking in tests that contain delay() makes the test suite extremely slow (a 1-second delay in 100 tests = 100 seconds of CI wall time) while runTest executes the same delays in milliseconds of real time.

Implementing a production-grade database inspection and debugging workflow for Android uses Database Inspector in Android Studio and the room-paging artifact for runtime query analysis. In development, enabling WAL journal mode on the Room database (database.query('PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL').close()) improves read concurrency and enables the Database Inspector to attach without blocking. For production database debugging, exporting the database file to accessible storage with database.close() followed by file copy to Downloads (requires READ_EXTERNAL_STORAGE + WRITE_EXTERNAL_STORAGE permissions or MediaStore) allows analysis with SQLiteOnline or DB Browser. Never ship database export functionality in production builds without authentication gating.

Hilt's @AndroidEntryPoint on Fragment requires the parent Activity to also be annotated with @AndroidEntryPoint or an exception is thrown at runtime. The hidden failure is a multi-module project where a feature Fragment is annotated with @AndroidEntryPoint but the host Activity (in the app module or a different feature module) lacks the annotation—this causes a compile-time error only when all modules are assembled together in the app module, not when feature modules are compiled independently. The constraint is that @AndroidEntryPoint annotation must be present on the entire Activity→Fragment chain, making Activities and Fragments tightly coupled through Hilt's component hierarchy even across module boundaries.

Implementing CI performance testing for Android that tracks app size regressions uses bundletools to extract the download size of the APK set from an App Bundle, integrated into the CI pipeline. The assembly is: build release AAB, run bundletool build-apks --bundle=app.aab --output=app.apks --mode=default, then bundletool get-size total --apks=app.apks to get the estimated download size. A GitHub Actions check that fails when the download size increases by more than a threshold (e.g., 500KB) prevents accidental dependency additions from bloating the app. Module-level size tracking with AGP's bundleReleaseResources task output reveals which resources contribute most to APK size.

Android's Jetpack Compose Modifier.clickable() does not provide visual feedback on older Android versions without a Ripple—missing the indication parameter causes invisible touch feedback. The hidden failure is implementing clickable composables with indication=null to avoid ripple flicker during animations, then shipping a product where users cannot tell which elements are interactive because there is no visual affordance. The correct approach for ripple-free feedback is providing a custom indication through Modifier.clickable(indication=null, interactionSource=remember{MutableInteractionSource()}) only for elements with clear visual state changes (like toggle buttons), while using the default ripple for navigation buttons and list items.

The Android Testing Strategy pyramid recommends a 70/20/10 split between unit, integration, and E2E tests. The rationale for heavy unit test investment in Android specifically is that instrumented tests (running on device/emulator) are 10-100x slower than JVM unit tests and require physical or virtual device provisioning in CI. A ViewModel that can be tested with pure Kotlin runTest without Android runtime dependencies runs in milliseconds; the equivalent Espresso test requires emulator boot time (30-120s per CI run) plus test execution time. Domain layer logic tested entirely with pure JVM tests enables faster feedback loops and higher test coverage without the cost of device farm time.

Implementing secure API key storage in Android apps uses a combination of obfuscation and runtime key assembly rather than storing keys in plaintext strings. The assembly pattern is: split the key into segments stored in separate string resources or computed from BuildConfig values, reassemble at runtime in native code (via JNI/NDK) to avoid easy string extraction from the APK. Keys should never appear as complete strings in Java/Kotlin bytecode. For the highest security requirement, use remote key delivery where the server provides a session-scoped API key after device attestation (Play Integrity), so the key never resides persistently on the device.

Jetpack Compose's @Preview annotation limitations include: previews cannot show composables that use LocalContext.current for reading system services (network state, sensors), previews fail when composables call LocalLifecycleOwner.current without a preview-compatible lifecycle provider, and previews using viewModel() without PreviewParameterProvider injection throw an exception in the preview renderer. The practical constraint is that every composable that uses context-aware APIs needs to be testable with injected fake implementations to be previewable. Designing composables to accept data as parameters rather than fetching from context enables richer previews and better testability simultaneously.

Implementing a complete in-app notification center (beyond push notifications) in Android requires a local database table for notification records, a ViewModel that loads unread count and notification list, and a Compose notification drawer UI that marks items as read on display. The assembly uses Room for local storage, with a NotificationEntity containing id, title, body, isRead, createdAt, deepLinkUrl. Marking all visible items as read uses LazyColumn's LaunchedEffect that fires on each item becoming visible, updating isRead=true via ViewModel. The unread count badge on the navigation icon uses Flow<Int> from a Room @Query(SELECT COUNT(*) FROM notifications WHERE isRead=0) collected in the main ViewModel.

Android's Notification channels created with importance IMPORTANCE_DEFAULT do not show heads-up notifications (floating banners)—IMPORTANCE_HIGH is required for heads-up. The hidden failure is creating notification channels during app development with IMPORTANCE_DEFAULT, then updating the importance to IMPORTANCE_HIGH in a later release, but Android only respects the importance level set at channel creation time; once a channel is created the importance cannot be changed programmatically. Users can manually change importance in Settings, but the channel must be deleted and recreated (with a new channel ID) to ship a higher importance level to existing users who have the old channel registered.

Kotlin's Arrow library provides functional programming abstractions (Either, Option, Validated, IO) that handle error propagation and null safety through type-level encoding rather than exceptions. The rationale for using Arrow's Either<Error, Success> over Kotlin's Result type in complex domain logic is that Either enables typed error hierarchies—Either<DomainError, User> where DomainError is a sealed class with specific subtypes gives callers exhaustive error type information. Kotlin's Result only distinguishes success from Throwable, losing error type information at the call site. The tradeoff is Arrow's learning curve and the idiom difference from standard Kotlin, making it most appropriate for teams committed to functional style throughout the codebase.

Implementing an Android Wear OS companion app with data sync uses the Wearable Data Layer API with DataClient for small data payloads (<100KB) and ChannelClient for streaming larger data. The assembly is: register WearableListenerService in both phone and watch manifests, use DataClient.putDataItem() with DataMapItem containing sync data keyed by URI path, and detect changes via onDataChanged(). For real-time health data streaming from watch to phone, ChannelClient provides a bidirectional byte stream channel better suited to continuous data. The phone and watch apps must share the same application ID and signing certificate for the Wearable Data Layer to connect them—mismatched signing fails silently with no error from DataClient.

Android's Foreground Service type requirement introduced in Android 14 (API 34) requires declaring the specific foreground service type (camera, microphone, location, mediaPlayback, etc.) in the manifest AND requesting the corresponding type-specific permission before calling startForeground(). The failure is upgrading targetSdk to 34 without auditing existing foreground services—each foreground service must declare its type and the app must have the associated runtime permission granted before calling startForeground(). Calling startForeground() with a type that the app does not have permission for throws a SecurityException that crashes the service immediately, breaking features that relied on that foreground service.

Implementing a production-ready ViewModel factory for ViewModels that require non-standard constructor parameters (without Hilt) uses the ViewModelProvider.Factory interface with CreationExtras. The assembly for a parameterized ViewModel is: object MyViewModelFactory : ViewModelProvider.Factory { override fun <T : ViewModel> create(modelClass: Class<T>, extras: CreationExtras): T { val application = extras[APPLICATION_KEY]!!; val savedStateHandle = extras.createSavedStateHandle(); return MyViewModel(application, savedStateHandle, myRepository) as T } }. Using by viewModels { MyViewModelFactory } in the Fragment passes the factory to the ViewModelProvider. Hilt eliminates this boilerplate entirely for most cases, but manual factories remain necessary for parameterized ViewModels in non-Hilt projects.

Kotlin Serialization (@Serializable) is the native serialization solution for Kotlin Multiplatform projects because it generates platform-specific serializers for each target (JVM, JS, Native) without reflection. The rationale for preferring Kotlin Serialization over Gson/Moshi in new Kotlin projects is that it supports Kotlin-specific features (sealed classes, default parameter values, value classes) that Gson handles incorrectly and Moshi requires custom adapters for. The compile-time code generation means serialization performance is on par with hand-written adapters. For Android-only projects, the choice between Moshi and Kotlin Serialization is largely stylistic; for KMM projects, Kotlin Serialization is the only reflection-free option that works on all targets.

Implementing Jetpack Compose's pull-to-refresh for lazy lists uses PullToRefreshBox from Material3 1.3.0+, wrapping the LazyColumn. The assembly is: val pullToRefreshState = rememberPullToRefreshState(); PullToRefreshBox(isRefreshing=isLoading, onRefresh={ viewModel.refresh() }, state=pullToRefreshState) { LazyColumn { ... } }. The ViewModel's refresh() function sets isLoading=true, fetches data, then sets isLoading=false via StateFlow. The hidden detail is that isRefreshing must be set to false after the data is loaded—if it stays true indefinitely due to an uncaught exception, the refresh indicator spins forever. Always wrap the refresh network call in a try-finally block that ensures isLoading is set to false in the finally clause.

Android's TaskStackBuilder for creating back-navigable notification PendingIntents must use TaskStackBuilder.addParentStack() or addNextIntentWithParentStack() to synthesize the entire activity back stack, because a notification launched directly into a deep screen without a proper back stack leaves the user stranded with no back button destination. The constraint is that the parent activities must have android:parentActivityName declared in the manifest, and TaskStackBuilder uses this declaration to construct the synthetic back stack. Without the synthesized stack, pressing back from a deep notification destination either closes the app entirely or goes to an unexpected screen, violating the Notification Design Guidelines and confusing users.

Android's Jetpack Compose recomposition skipping for composables that receive lambda parameters does not work unless the lambda is stable. Passing a lambda that captures mutable state (val onClick = { viewModel.handleClick() }) to a child composable causes the child to recompose on every parent recomposition because the lambda captures a new instance each time, making equality checks fail. The fix is wrapping lambdas with remember { { viewModel.handleClick() } } for stable capture, or using Kotlin's method references which produce stable instances. For complex lambdas with captured state, defining them as stable @Stable annotated interfaces with rememberUpdatedState for the captured values prevents unnecessary recomposition.

Implementing a color picker in Compose for customization features requires either using a third-party library (github.com/skydoves/colorpicker-compose) or a custom Canvas-based HSV picker. The custom assembly uses HSVtoColor() for the gradient rendering—the hue bar is drawn as a LinearGradient across the hue range, the saturation-value square uses two overlapping gradients (white-to-hue horizontally, transparent-to-black vertically). Touch handling with Modifier.pointerInput computes the hue/saturation/value from the touch position relative to the picker bounds. Color.hsv() requires importing compose.ui.graphics and using the HSV color space explicitly since Android's default Color is in RGB space.

Android's MediaCodec API is the low-level hardware-accelerated video encoding/decoding API that ExoPlayer and CameraX use internally. The rationale for using CameraX VideoCapture over raw MediaCodec for video recording is that MediaCodec configuration requires handling surface synchronization, encoder input/output buffer management, and muxer integration (MediaMuxer for MP4 output)—a production-grade video recorder requires 500-1000 lines of error-prone MediaCodec code that CameraX provides as a 50-line use case. The MediaCodec API is only appropriate when frame-by-frame processing or non-standard codecs are required (custom H.265 encoding parameters, AV1 encoding for streaming optimization).

Kotlin's data class copy() function creates a shallow copy—nested mutable objects are shared between the original and the copy. The failure manifests in ViewModel state management where the UiState data class contains a list property copied with state.copy(items=newItems), but if items is a MutableList shared with a previous state snapshot, mutations to that list after the copy corrupt the previous state's reference. The invariant is: always use immutable List (not MutableList) in UI state data classes, and always create new list instances rather than modifying existing ones. This ensures that StateFlow emissions of different state snapshots are truly independent objects.

Implementing Android app shortcuts (static shortcuts and dynamic shortcuts) uses ShortcutManagerCompat for backward-compatible shortcut creation. The assembly is: static shortcuts defined in shortcuts.xml with ShortcutInfoCompat including id, shortLabel (max 10 chars), longLabel (max 25 chars), icon, and intent; dynamic shortcuts created with ShortcutManagerCompat.pushDynamicShortcut() for recent items (limited to 4-5 shortcuts by the OS). The critical detail is that shortcut intents must point to the Activity that handles the deep link, not the launcher Activity, so the shortcut takes the user directly to the intended screen without passing through onboarding. ShortcutInfoCompat.Builder requires a context with application context to avoid memory leaks from the builder holding an Activity reference.

The Android Gradle Plugin's configuration caching feature (stable from AGP 8.1) serializes the task graph configuration phase to disk and skips re-running it on subsequent builds when inputs have not changed. The rationale for enabling configuration cache in CI is that the Gradle configuration phase for large Android projects (100+ modules) takes 30-90 seconds on every build, and configuration caching reduces this to 1-2 seconds for cached builds. The tradeoff is that Gradle plugins must be configuration-cache compatible—many older plugin versions throw ConfigurationCacheException when this is enabled, requiring plugin upgrades. The production recommendation is enabling configuration cache with --configuration-cache flag in CI first before forcing it in project settings.

Implementing comprehensive instrumented testing with Espresso for critical user flows uses Espresso's IdlingResource to synchronize test execution with background operations. The assembly for testing a list that loads from network is: create a CountingIdlingResource, increment before every network request, decrement in every response callback, register with IdlingRegistry.getInstance().register(idlingResource) in @Before and unregister in @After. Espresso automatically waits for all registered IdlingResources to reach idle count=0 before proceeding with the next test statement. Without IdlingResource, test assertions run before the network response arrives, producing flaky tests that sometimes pass (fast network) and sometimes fail (slow network).
